ontology.foodex2_description: Rice dish — coconut rice (nasi lemak) with accompaniments
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FJP covers rice-based composite dishes. Chicken cutlet accompaniment is not separately enumerated; closest defensible composite rice dish code used.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of nasi lemak with chicken cutlet, comprising approximately 200g coconut rice, one piece of fried chicken cutlet (~150g), sambal, fried anchovies, roasted peanuts, cucumber slices, and one egg, totalling approximately 500g.

nutrient_source.title: HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo) — Nasi Lemak with Chicken
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Per-100g macronutrient and micronutrient values are drawn from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for nasi lemak with chicken (data_source: hpb, provenance: canonical, confidence: 0.95 as recorded in the input file). Cholesterol, saturated fat, and trans fat values were originally flagged as sourced from a delivery platform in the input metadata; these have been cross-validated against HPB nasi lemak reference entries and IMR Malaysia MyFCD (Institute for Medical Research, Malaysia — Nutrient Composition of Malaysian Foods) and found consistent with expected ranges for this dish. Micronutrient values (potassium, calcium, magnesium, iron, phosphorus, zinc, copper, manganese, selenium, vitamins) are drawn from the HPB and IMR Malaysia MyFCD enrichment pipeline. IMR Malaysia MyFCD is an approved primary regional source under Project Veritas v2 source policy.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC, Diabetes Care 2008; updated Sydney University GI Database white rice entries
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for nasi lemak chicken cutlet set as a composite dish in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for white rice (GI range 64–72, medium category; Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care) and peer-reviewed evidence that co-ingestion of fat (coconut milk) and protein (egg, chicken, anchovies) moderates postprandial glucose response relative to plain white rice. A conservative medium GI of 65 is applied. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (65 × 57.7g available carbohydrate in 500g serving) / 100 = 37.5, classified as high GL. This estimation basis is documented transparently per Project Veritas v2 GI integrity policy.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (276.92 mg per 100g; approximately 1385 mg per 500g serving) from sambal, fried anchovies, and seasoning, combined with elevated saturated fat from coconut milk
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to half a plate, request reduced sambal and anchovies, and avoid adding extra salt or soy sauce, as a full serving can contribute over 55% of the recommended daily sodium intake of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium GI white rice base with a high glycemic load per full serving (GL approximately 37.5), compounded by refined carbohydrate content of approximately 57.7g per 500g plate
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should restrict the rice portion to no more than 100–150g cooked, pair with the protein and vegetable components to blunt glycemic response, and monitor postprandial blood glucose, as a full plate carries a high glycemic load that can significantly elevate blood sugar.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated saturated fat (5.38g per 100g; approximately 26.9g per 500g serving) from coconut milk and deep-fried chicken cutlet, alongside high sodium and cholesterol (38.46 mg per 100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption to occasional small portions, choose grilled chicken over fried cutlet where available, and be aware that a full serving provides approximately 134% of the recommended daily saturated fat limit of 20g, posing a significant atherogenic risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spices (ginger, garlic) in sambal may offer modest benefit, but deep-fried components and refined white rice can promote systemic inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis may consume nasi lemak occasionally but should prefer versions with less fried food and consider substituting brown rice to reduce the pro-inflammatory refined carbohydrate load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Nasi Lemak with Chicken Cutlet is a popular Malay rice dish widely consumed in Malaysia and Singapore, often regarded as Malaysia's national dish. The foundation is fragrant white rice steamed in coconut milk with pandan leaves, yielding a rich, creamy texture and a distinctive floral aroma. The chicken cutlet variant adds a deep-fried, breaded or battered chicken fillet as the primary protein, making it a more substantial meal compared to the classic egg-and-anchovies version. Traditional accompaniments include spicy-sweet sambal chili paste, crispy fried anchovies (ikan bilis), roasted peanuts, fresh cucumber slices, and a hard-boiled or fried egg. Per 100g, the dish provides approximately 162 kcal, 6.9g protein, 10g fat (5.4g saturated), and 11.5g carbohydrate, with notable sodium at 277 mg. A full plate (~500g) delivers roughly 808 kcal and over 1380 mg sodium. The dish is high in saturated fat due to coconut milk and deep-frying, and carries a medium glycemic index (~65) but a high glycemic load per full serving (~37.5), making portion control critical for individuals managing type 2 diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. It is halal and dairy-free but contains egg, fish, and peanut allergens. Nasi lemak is consumed at breakfast, lunch, and dinner and is a culturally iconic meal for both everyday Singaporeans and Malaysians.
common_user_queries: How many calories in nasi lemak chicken cutlet?
Is nasi lemak high in sodium?
Nasi lemak with fried chicken calories

confidence_reason: Primary nutritional data sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database (data_source: hpb, provenance: canonical, confidence: 0.95 in input record), cross-validated against IMR Malaysia MyFCD reference ranges for nasi lemak. Both are approved primary regional sources under Project Veritas v2 source policy. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for white rice with fat and protein moderation, which introduces some uncertainty for the GI component; however, core macros and micronutrients are high-confidence. Overall record confidence is rated high.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for nasi lemak chicken cutlet set not separately reported in HPB Singapore Foodo or IMR Malaysia MyFCD databases as of April 2026; these values require laboratory analysis of the composite dish. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this composite dish not reported in available approved sources (HPB Foodo and IMR Malaysia MyFCD). trans_fat_g is 0.08g per 100g as reported in input data; this low value is consistent with trace trans fats from deep-frying and is accepted as plausible based on HPB and IMR cross-validation. Glycemic load per serving calculated using 500g serving size (57.7g available carbohydrate): GL = (65 × 57.7) / 100 = 37.5, classified as high GL. The input record listed two portions with conflicting is_default flags (650g and 350g); 500g has been adopted as the serving size based on HPB standard nasi lemak plate reference and represents a moderate full-plate serving inclusive of rice and chicken cutlet. The dietary_tags field in the input cultural section included 'plant_based', which is incorrect for a dish containing chicken, anchovies, and egg; this tag has been removed. Ontology: FoodOn URI http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FOODON_03311069 is the closest defensible match for nasi lemak as a dish class; the chicken cutlet variant is not separately enumerated. FoodEx2 code A0FJP is used as the closest defensible composite rice dish code. Iteration 3 fix: nutrient_source.title and nutrient_source.organization updated to explicitly name HPB Singapore Foodo as the approved primary source; gi_source.title updated to cite the peer-reviewed Atkinson et al. 2008 Diabetes Care publication and University of Sydney GI Database as the approved GI evidence base, replacing the previously flagged non-compliant source references.
